3: The global demand for products of animal origin has been steadily increasing over the past 50 years driven by rapid popula- tion and income growth and it is expected to grow further in the

near future (FAO, 2018). At the same time, animal food products are key drivers of adverse environmental effects such as climate change, water and air pollution, land degradation and biodiversity loss, to name a few (Poore and Nemecek, 2018; Crenna et al., 2019). The poultry industry is strongly linked to these phenomena since today it appears the most developed among the supply chains of animal products and is experiencing continuous growth that shows no signs of stopping. Although the poultry supply chain has been identified by several authors as the most environmentally efficient

CC|Climate Change CP|Crude Protein FCR|Feed Conversion Ratio FE|Freshwater Eutrophication FEx|Freshwater Ecotoxicity FU|Functional Unit HTc|Human toxicity, cancer effects HTnoc|Human toxicity, non-cancer effects LCA|Life Cycle Assessment LUC|Land Use Change LW|Live Weight ME|Marine Eutrophication MFRD|Mineral, Fossil and renewable Resource Depletion OD|Ozone Depletion PM|Particulate Matter formation POF|Photochemical Oxidant Formation TA|Terrestrial Acidification TE|Terrestrial Eutrophication
among those of animal products (Roma et al., 2015), raising con- cerns about the environmental sustainability of livestock pro- ductions have put even this sector under investigation.

As regards in particular the chicken egg production, in 2017 it accounted globally for 80.0 Mt, the maximum production ever reached. The European Union (EU-28), with 7.1 Mt produced in the same year, contributes significantly to the market for this product (FAOSTAT, 2020). Italy ranks among the most important European egg producers: in 2018, it hosted a population of about 39 million laying hens and the national production reached about 12.6 billion eggs, which made the country self-sufficient for eggs demand (ISMEA, 2019).

With regard to the rearing systems, in the EU countries, until the early 2000s the majority of egg farms practiced rearing of laying hens in cages, while recently alternative housing methods are spreading, mainly due to a growing public concern for animal welfare. A survey conducted by the European Union, in fact, in- dicates that an absolute majority of citizens (94%) are of the view it is important to protect the welfare of farmed animals (Special Eurobarometer 442, 2016). Moreover, most respondents (59%) indicate a higher willingness-to-pay for improved animal welfare. Referring specifically to laying hens, some recent studies (Leenstra et al., 2014; Zakowska-Biemans and Tekien, 2017; Rahmani et al., 2019) have shown that European consumers may consider free- range farming systems positively influencing animal welfare, which explains the increase in consumer demand for eggs deriving from cage-free facilities. Furthermore, the European Union ban on conventional cage rearing (Council Directive, 1999/74/EC) has forced many farmers to shift towards different housing techniques. Still in accordance with EU Council Directive 1999/74/EC, the currently accepted rearing systems of laying hens are furnished cages, more commonly known as enriched, and three forms of alternative systems (cage-free), namely barn, free-range and organic.

In 2018, Italian egg production was divided as follows: 45% came from barn systems, 42% from enriched cages, 9% from organic and 3% from free-range systems. However, market trends in recent years show that sales of eggs from caged hens are continuously decreasing at the expense of those from alternative systems (ISMEA, 2019).
Generally, organic farming claims to lighten the environmental

burden of agricultural production while generating ecological benefits. However, organic agriculture is accused of being less productive than traditional agriculture, and this efficiency gap is often identified negatively from an environmental point of view (Clark and Tilman, 2017). As concerns egg production, the organic system generally appears to be less efficient of the other rearing systems, particularly of cages. According to Roos et al. (2018), organic hens usually show a higher feed conversion ratio (FCR) between +2% and +20% compared to hens housed in cages or aviaries, and commonly present even an increased mortality due to injuries and diseases. Considering the important role of feed on the overall impact (Clune et al., 2017), many authors agree that organic eggs perform worse than non-organic in environmental terms, while on the other side there is who claims that the production gap is compensated by low input of resources to the system (Leinonen and Kyriazakys, 2016). However, both sides show a degree of vari- ability in literature and there is not one that performs better than the other with consistency, nor for all impact categories in the same way (Leinonen et al., 2012; Pelletier, 2017).

Up to now, authors have conducted studies on this topic mainly in order to track a representative egg environmental performance for a regional or a country production, namely for Sweden (Cederberg et al., 2009), Australia (Wiedemann and McGahan, 2010), Netherlands (Dekker et al., 2011), United Kingdom (Leinonen et al., 2012), Iowa (USA) (Pelletier et al., 2013), Alborz (Iran) (Ghasempour and Ahmadi, 2016) and Canada (Pelletier, 2017). Some of these studies additionally aimed to compare different rearing systems and verify their influence on environ- mental performance. In particular, the organic system was included in the analysis by Dekker et al. (2011), Leinonen et al. (2012) and Pelletier (2017). Abín et al. (2018) instead, investigated the impacts of a single rearing system (i.e. enriched cages) in Spain. Van Hal et al. (2019) performed an LCA study on the impact of egg pro- duction by exploring an innovative allocation method that rewards low-opportunity-cost-feedstuffs as they avoid feed-food competi- tion. Compared to other livestock sectors, however, studies on the environmental impact of egg production are limited.

All the previously quoted studies adopted the environmental Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach. LCA, whose normative reference is represented by ISO 14040 (ISO, 2006) and 14044 (ISO, 2018), is a method originally developed for industrial processes but recently has also been commonly employed in the agri-food sector (Lovarelli and Bacenetti, 2017a; Bernardi et al., 2018; Tedesco et al., 2019). LCA allows to quantify the potential environmental impact of a product (or process) during its whole life cycle, or a part of it, and to identify hotspots within the examined systems, thus to generate targeted mitigation strategies (Notarnicola et al., 2017). In the Italian context, although several livestock systems have already been analysed with the environmental LCA approach, there is a lack of studies related to egg production. This is the first study focused on the environmental performance of organic egg production in Italy and therefore aims to: quantify its impact in order to provide a national reference; highlight the main contributors to this impact, also referred to as environmental hotspots; starting from these, discuss possible mitigation strategies, highlighting any environ- mental critical issues of the system and rooms for improvement.

10: Based on the gaps identified in the introduction, this LCA study was performed with the following aims:

<list>quantify the environmental impact related to the production of eggs according with the organic method in Northern Italy; 
identify the processes mainly responsible for this impact and, consequently, discuss some potential mitigation solutions that could be implemented for impact reduction; 
strike up a debate on the influence of the rearing system on the environmental impact of egg production, with a keen eye on main parameters that determine differences between them.</list>

The analysis is based on data collected from a farm specialized in organic egg production located in the North-Eastern Italian region of Friuli-Venezia Giulia, which, given its characteristics, can be considered representative of this egg production system in the Po valley.

The outcomes of this study could be useful for the stakeholders involved in the poultry industry (e.g. feed producers, farmers and processors) to have a better understanding of the environmental performance of the supply chain they work in. At the same time, the results could support actions and/or policies to raise environmental awareness and sustainability by farmers, as well as by individuals, organizations, associations and policymakers.

12: The analysed farm rears laying hens in an organic farming sys- tem, following the indications of Council Regulations (EC) n. 834/ 2007 and 889/2008 on organic production. The production cycle begins with the purchase of a 3000 pullets flock, raised with organic method in another specialized farm that is located in the neighboring region. This flock corresponds to the maximum number of animals that can be raised simultaneously in a poultry house for laying hens, according to the aforementioned European regulations on organic production. Pullets are imported at 16 weeks of age and a live weight (LW) of 1.55 kg, ready to start the laying cycle. In fact, the analysed farm manages only the egg- laying phase, which lasts 72 weeks. During this period, animals spend part of the time inside the barn, and they also have free daily access to a free-range area, normally allowed by the farmer during daylight hours. The barn has an area of 500 m², which is partly covered with sand as litter material, and is also equipped with a raised tier with a slatted floor, on which automatic feeders, perches and special nests are placed. Eggs are collected from nests with automatic belts. The free-range is 1.25 ha and is cultivated with alfalfa, which is renewed every 3 years. In detail, the mechanized operations carried out are plowing and harrowing, seed bed preparation, and subsequently sowing and rolling. No other inter- vention is carried out during the 3 years. Since the farm does not own any land other than that destined for the free-range area, the entire feed supply is purchased externally by a specialized feed mill authorized in organic feed production. During the laying cycle, two different feeds are provided: the first with a crude protein (CP) content of 18.5% for a period of 24 weeks, and the second with 17% CP until the end of the cycle, for a total of 48 weeks. These two commercial feeds are produced locally, with raw agricultural materias of Italian origin only, and are mainly composed by maize and wheat grains, wheat bran and soybean meal.

At the end of the productive cycle, at a LW of 1.95 kg, spent hens are sent to the slaughterhouse. Then, following the all-in/all-out principle, a depopulation period of 21 days is carried out before a new batch of pullets is brought in. In this time frame, the barn is completely cleansed with steam and the whole amount of manure (40 tons) produced during the cycle is collected. The manure is entirely exported to a nearby cereal farm producing maize grain, which forms part of the maize used as feed ingredient.

14: The Functional Unit (FU) is the unit to which refer all the in- ventory data and the environmental results. In this study, 1 kg of fresh shelled eggs produced with organic method was selected as FU, in accordance with Livestock Environmental Assessment and Performance Partnership (LEAP) guidelines (LEAP, 2016).

The study was carried out in a cradle-to-farm gate perspective, thus including in the system boundary the eggs life cycle until the end of the agricultural phase (Fig. Therefore, all inputs related to the rearing cycle, such as the production, purchase and on-farm transportation of feed and pullets, as well as the supply of other inputs such as electricity, water and litter material, were consid- ered. On the contrary, packaging, from-farm transport, possible processing, market distribution and consumption phases, and all related waste disposal were excluded.

As crop intended for feed production is concerned, this assess- ment considered raw material extraction (e.g., fossil fuels and minerals); manufacture (seeds, fertilizers and agricultural ma- chines); use (diesel fuel consumption and engines exhaust gas emissions, tire abrasion emissions and fertilizer related emissions); maintenance and final disposal of agricultural machines.
Emissions due to feces deposition during animal housing have been included in the assessment, as well as the impact related to manure export and its field spreading.

Due to the lack of information, similarly to previous LCA studies on poultry productions (Roma et al., 2015), impacts related to drug use were excluded, and the same was done for production and maintenance of farm's facilities and devices.

As concerns agricultural land directly and indirectly involved in the analysis, no changes in the soil organic carbon were taken into account because considered to be in a steady state. In fact, ac- cording to the legislation on organic agricultural products, all feed ingredients are produced locally, thus on agricultural land of the Po Valley destined for arable crops where no land use change (LUC) has occurred recently. This assumption agrees with previous studies on the soil carbon stock topic (Huang et al., 2012; Han et al., 2016) and with previous LCA analyses focused on agricultural production in Northern Italy (Noya et al., 2018; Bacenetti, 2019).

As crop intended for feed production is concerned, this assess- ment considered raw material extraction (e.g., fossil fuels and minerals); manufacture (seeds, fertilizers and agricultural ma- chines); use (diesel fuel consumption and engines exhaust gas emissions, tire abrasion emissions and fertilizer related emissions); maintenance and final disposal of agricultural machines.
Emissions due to feces deposition during animal housing have been included in the assessment, as well as the impact related to manure export and its field spreading.

Due to the lack of information, similarly to previous LCA studies on poultry productions (Roma et al., 2015), impacts related to drug use were excluded, and the same was done for production and maintenance of farm's facilities and devices.

As concerns agricultural land directly and indirectly involved in the analysis, no changes in the soil organic carbon were taken into account because considered to be in a steady state. In fact, ac- cording to the legislation on organic agricultural products, all feed ingredients are produced locally, thus on agricultural land of the Po Valley destined for arable crops where no land use change (LUC) has occurred recently. This assumption agrees with previous studies on the soil carbon stock topic (Huang et al., 2012; Han et al., 2016) and with previous LCA analyses focused on agricultural production in Northern Italy (Noya et al., 2018; Bacenetti, 2019).

feeds, detailed information on field operations and related inputs and outputs were recovered by Bacenetti et al. (2016), Bacenetti et al. (2017a), Dal Ferro et al. (2017) as regards maize, wheat and soybean; Bacenetti et al. (2017b) as for flax and Forleo et al. (2018) for sunflower. These studies reflect peculiarities of the Italian agricultural context for the different feed ingredients production. However, to ensure the consistency with the system under study, i.e. to comply with organic production specifications, mineral fer- tilizers inputs and relative distribution operations were not included in the inventory. The same was done with pesticides, which were replaced with mechanical weeding interventions, for which data were taken from Lovarelli and Bacenetti (2017b) and Lovarelli et al. (2017). Finally, to take into account the significant drop in yields normally observed in organic farming (De Ponti et al., 2012), a yield ratio of 70% was assumed for all the cultures with respect to the conventional farming references, similarly to the average value observed by Dal Ferro et al. (2017) in Northern Italy.
Background data relating to feed processing, production of

pullets, electricity, water, litter and on-farm transports were ob- tained from the established life cycle inventories data sources Ecoinvent V3.5 (Weidema et al., 2013; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2018) and Agrifootprint (Blonk Consultants, 2014). All electricity consump- tions, both direct on the farm and those related to upstream pro- cesses, were modeled using the medium voltage Italian energy mix at grid. Impact due to the on-farm transport of pullets, litter ma- terial and feed inputs was computed through the ton-per-kilometer method, knowing the distance from the suppliers.

Emissions related to animal housing and manure management were estimated by following the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006, 2019); in particular, Tier 2 was used to quantify methane (CH4) emissions and Tier 1 for nitrous oxide (N20) and ammonia (NH3). Methane emissions from enteric fermentations were assumed to be zero, in consistence with previous LCA studies on poultry systems, both when intended for the production of eggs (Leinonen et al., 2012; Pelletier, 2017) and broiler meat (Cesari et al., 2017; Da Silva et al., 2019).

In order to estimate CH4 emissions from manure, volatile solids excretion was predicted using the equation n. 5 provided by LEAP (2016). Due to the lack of specific data, the diet digestibility was considered to be fixed at 80% and manure ash content at 10% (LEAP, 2016). Subsequently, a methane producing capacity factor (Bo) of 0.39 m³ CH4/kg of volatile solids and a methane conversion factor of 1.5% were used for computing manure CH4 emissions (IPCC, 2006, 2019).

The excreted nitrogen, required to estimate nitrogen com- pounds emissions, was calculated by deducting nitrogen retention from nitrogen intake. The latter was derived from the dry matter intake of the animals and the diet CP content, both provided by the farmer. The retention instead was predicted using the equation n. 10.33D provided by IPCC (2006; 2019), thus considering egg mass production, average nitrogen content in eggs (1.85% on mass), daily weight gain and average nitrogen content in LW (2.8% on mass).

Subsequently, nitrous oxide and ammonia emission were calculated using the Tier 1 conversion factors of IPCC (2006; 2019). For both nitrogen compounds, specific conversion factors were used for the interior of the barn and the free-range area. In this regard, considering the average time spent by the hens in the different areas of the farm, it has been assumed that the animals deposit 70% of the feces inside and 30% outside. For the share deposited externally, nitrate (NO) leaching was calculated ac- cording with Brentrup et al. (2000) considering as if it were spread manure.

As in the majority of LCA studies focused on livestock systems (Roma et al., 2015), CO2 emitted by the animals was not accounted because considered balanced by that fixed by means of photosyn- thesis by the plants involved in the system, i.e. crops (IPCC, 2006).
Further information on crop production elaboration (Tables S1, S2, S3, S4, S5) and emissions estimation (Table S6) can be found in the supplementary materials.

The latter was derived from the dry matter intake of the animals and the diet CP content, both provided by the farmer. The retention instead was predicted using the equation n. 10.33D provided by IPCC (2006; 2019), thus considering egg mass production, average nitrogen content in eggs (1.85% on mass), daily weight gain and average nitrogen content in LW (2.8% on mass).

Subsequently, nitrous oxide and ammonia emission were calculated using the Tier 1 conversion factors of IPCC (2006; 2019). For both nitrogen compounds, specific conversion factors were used for the interior of the barn and the free-range area. In this regard, considering the average time spent by the hens in the different areas of the farm, it has been assumed that the animals deposit 70% of the feces inside and 30% outside. For the share deposited externally, nitrate (NO) leaching was calculated ac- cording with Brentrup et al. (2000) considering as if it were spread manure.

As in the majority of LCA studies focused on livestock systems (Roma et al., 2015), CO2 emitted by the animals was not accounted because considered balanced by that fixed by means of photosyn- thesis by the plants involved in the system, i.e. crops (IPCC, 2006).
Further information on crop production elaboration (Tables S1, S2, S3, S4, S5) and emissions estimation (Table S6) can be found in the supplementary materials.

Emissions related to animal housing and manure management were estimated by following the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006, 2019); in particular, Tier 2 was used to quantify methane (CH4) emissions and Tier 1 for nitrous oxide (N20) and ammonia (NH3). For the share deposited externally, nitrate (NO) leaching was calculated ac- cording with Brentrup et al. (2000) considering as if it were spread manure.

As in the majority of LCA studies focused on livestock systems (Roma et al., 2015), CO2 emitted by the animals was not accounted because considered balanced by that fixed by means of photosyn- thesis by the plants involved in the system, i.e. crops (IPCC, 2006).

23: When multifunctional processes are studied, allocation should be introduced in order to assign impact shares to the different co- products. As the function of the system under analysis in this study is to supply eggs, the environmental impacts were not allo- cated but attributed entirely to eggs, by far the most relevant product of the system both in economic, mass and nutritional value terms. However, to check if this methodological choice had a sig- nificant effect on the results, a sensitivity analysis was carried out which explored the influence of other possible allocation methods, as suggested in LEAP guidelines. The setting of this analysis is dis- cussed in section 2.7.
24: In the life cycle impact assessment phase, emissions and resource data identified during the inventory are translated into indicators that reflect environment pressures as well as resource scarcity. The impact assessment was performed using the ILCD (International Reference Life Cycle Data System) 2011 Midpoint + method (V1.03), which is the impact assessment method endorsed by the European Commission, and processed with the SimaPro® 8.0.5 software. The evaluated impact categories are the following: climate change (CC), ozone depletion (OD), particulate matter formation (PM), photochemical oxidant forma- tion (POF), terrestrial acidification (TA), terrestrial eutrophication (TE), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), freshwater ecotoxicity (FEx), mineral and fossil resources depletion (MFRD).
25: A sensitivity analysis was carried out with the variation of some methodological choices and key parameters to investigate their effect on the environmental impact. The following aspects were taken in consideration:

<list>Allocation method. Results variation was evaluated when considering the meat of spent hens as a relevant co-product. In this regard, LEAP guidelines (2016) suggest that it would be preferable to handle this co-production using biophysical allo- cation. This was made by computing metabolizable energy re- quirements for growth and egg production. As a reference for these requirements, 5.5 kcal/g of LW gain (starting from the hatchling weight) and 2.07 kcal/g of egg were considered (NRC, 1994). In addition, economic allocation was evaluated, taking reference prices from the wholesale poultry exchange of Forlì, one of the most important for the sector in Northern Italy. The prices used were € 14.68/100 units for medium-sized organic eggs and € 0.27/kg of LW for medium-sized spent hens (aver- ages for January 2020, ISMEA, 2020). It should be noted that the value of spent hens' meat is much lower than that of broiler meat. Moreover, the rearing system from which spent hens come does not influence the meat price, as it is however considered low quality poultry meat. 
Hen-day egg production. Indeed, it is a key production param- eter, directly connected to the FCR, and which may possibly undergo variations due to environmental (temperature, hu- midity, etc.) and health (dysmetabolic diseases, leg injuries, etc.) conditions, and possibly due to the presence of other stressors, such as sporadic attacks by predators in the free-range area. The environmental performance was therefore explored for hen-day egg production of 85% (LOW scenario) and 93% (HIGH scenario). As a consequence to daily egg-laying changes, variations in an- fimals' nitrogen retention and excretion rates were taken into account, thus estimates of manure-related emissions were adapted.</list>
When cross-referencing both aspects, changes due to different total productions on the total value of eggs produced and energy requirements were also taken into consideration.

the relative contribution of the different inputs. Each column of the graph can be read as a pie chart, facilitating the identification of environmental hotspots. The input and output flows considered to make the analysis run are grouped for the results presentation into five classes: feed production (referring to consumption that takes place during the egg-laying cycle), pullets production, manure- related emissions, electricity (referring to on-farm consumption for lighting, automatic feeder and egg collection belts) and other inputs (referring to the sum of impacts due to on-farm transport of pullets, feed and litter; litter material production; water supply; free-range area management operations).
The impact of each class is discussed below:

<list>- Feed production is the main environmental hotspot, being the major driver of the impact for all the evaluated categories with a share ranging from 49% for TA and TE to 87% for HTnoc. This impact is related to energy requirements and machinery use for agricultural production and feed mill processing, as well as to emissions occurring during crop cultivation. Breaking the feed impact down between the two different feeds used, it emerges that the feed with 17% CP impacts for approximately two thirds (from 64% to 68% over all the evaluated impact categories) of the total, while the feed with 18.4% CP for the remainder. This is due to the fact that they have a similar composition but the first is used for a period of twice the duration of the other within the laying cycle therefore about twice the mass is consumed. 
- Manure-related emissions affect significantly CC (12%), PM (35%), TA (39%), TE (39%) and ME (14%). Among these, the manure-related impact is entirely due to NH3 volatilization for PM, TA and TE. NH3 is also responsible for 83% of the manure- related share for ME that, even if to a lesser extent (17%), is also influenced by NO3 leaching in the free-range area. Regarding the CC, the contribution of manure-related emissions is mainly due to N20, which represents 77% of the manure- related greenhouse gases emissions, while CH4 contributed the remaining 23%. 
- Pullets production is responsible for a share ranging from 10% to 14% for all the evaluated impact categories. This impact, in turn, is due especially to the feed consumption necessary for feeding the growing pullets in the specialized farm (Fig. S1). Other contributing factors are emissions related to manure and</list>

<list>- Electricity consumption on-farm has a limited contribution on the total impact for all the evaluated impact categories, resulting in less than 4% for all categories except for OD and FEx, for which it makes up respectively 6% and 11%. 
- Other inputs were grouped due to their small overall impact. Indeed, their share is below 2% for all the evaluated impact categories.</list>
housing management, electricity and heat consumptions and the supply of one-day-old chickens to the pullets farm.

Given the growing global concern on the climate change issue and its strong connection with the livestock sector (McClelland et al., 2018), specific attention is hereby paid to this aspect. Feed production affects the total CC impact (1.56 kg CO2 eq/kg eggs) for 72%, representing by far the major contributor, followed by manure-related emissions (12%), pullets production (11%), elec- tricity (4%) and other inputs (1%). Fig. 3 shows the results of a contribution analysis conducted on greenhouse gases.

The overall CC impact, expressed in kg CO2 eq., is caused mainly by CO2 emissions (58%), followed by N20 (34%) and, lastly, CH4 (8%) (Table S8). The effect of other substances is negligible (about 0.1%), thus not reported in the graph. Feed production is the main emitter for all the three gases (Table S9): huge amounts of CO2 are emitted during mechanized field operations and feed processing, while N20 and CH4 are emitted from organic fertilizers storage, handling and spreading on fields. Pullets production is the second contributor for CO2: manure-related emissions, on the other hand, represent a significant share of N20 and CH4.

Given the growing global concern on the climate change issue and its strong connection with the livestock sector (McClelland et al., 2018), specific attention is hereby paid to this aspect. Feed production affects the total CC impact (1.56 kg CO2 eq/kg eggs) for 72%, representing by far the major contributor, followed by manure-related emissions (12%), pullets production (11%), elec- tricity (4%) and other inputs (1%). Fig. 3 shows the results of a contribution analysis conducted on greenhouse gases.

The overall CC impact, expressed in kg CO2 eq., is caused mainly by CO2 emissions (58%), followed by N20 (34%) and, lastly, CH4 (8%) (Table S8). The effect of other substances is negligible (about 0.1%), thus not reported in the graph. Feed production is the main emitter for all the three gases (Table S9): huge amounts of CO2 are emitted during mechanized field operations and feed processing, while N20 and CH4 are emitted from organic fertilizers storage, handling and spreading on fields. Pullets production is the second contributor for CO2: manure-related emissions, on the other hand, represent a significant share of N20 and CH4.

significantly affect the results for CC, PM, POF, TA, TE, FE (coefficient of variation < 3%) and ME (coefficient of variation < 8%) while it has great influence on OD, the toxicity related impact categories and MFRD.
6: <header>4. Discussion </header>

The results clearly revealed feed production as the main hotspot for any impact category for the system under analysis. The primary role of feed in determining impacts and its relative contribution appears in line with previous LCA studies on egg production (Dekker 2011; Leinonen et al., 2012; Pelletier, 2017; Abín et al., 2018), and without particular differences attributable to geographic location or adopted rearing system. Also, pullets production and emissions related to manure contributed significantly. As concerns the substances contribution analysis of CC, the results obtained in this study are in accordance with Dekker et al. (2011) and Wiedemann and McGahan (2010), while other authors (e.g. Abín et al., 2018) identified N20 instead of CO2 as the main greenhouse gas emitted in terms of CO2 eq from the egg production system.

The absolute value for CC observed in this study for the selected FU (1.56 of CO2 eq/kg eggs) falls within the lower quartile of the values range identified in a recent literature review (1.3-6.0 eq/kg eggs, Clune et al., 2017). With the awareness that some of the variability observed between different LCA studies is due to methodological choices, the result still shows the analysed farm to have a good environmental performance in terms of carbon foot- print. The farm's productivity and the avoidance of some inputs that normally weigh heavily from an environmental point of view in conventional systems, such as mineral fertilizers and imported feed ingredients, have contributed to achieving this result. An important role in determining the impact was certainly played by the replacement of South American soybeans with Italian- produced one, thus excluding impacts related to long distances transportation and LUC at the expense of forest areas. The result also confirms that eggs, represent together with milk the most sustainable product of animal origin with regard to CC (De Vries and De Boer, 2010), beyond the production system adopted.

For the other impact categories it is difficult to make compari- sons because different assumptions were made on the model used for emissions estimation and/or on the characterization factors used (De Vries and De Boer, 2010), as well as because of the use of a different functional unit or system boundary. At the same time, it is important to consider a full set of impact categories to avoid impact shifting among them as well as a misrepresentation of trade-offs between impacts. This calls for comprehensive studies that cover a large spectrum of environmental impacts to avoid the previously mentioned burden-shifting in order to assess the environmental trade-offs between conventional and organic egg production systems.

The results show that mitigation interventions on feed pro- duction would be the most incisive option to reduce the overall impact, leading to an environmental improvement for all impact categories. What can be done at the farm level in this regard is to seek continuous improvement in FCR in order to lower feed con- sumption. The goal must be to optimize this consumption without sacrificing productivity. However, the environmental mitigation on feed production must also take place beyond the farm boundaries: it is essential that sustainable production starts from crops, adopting strategies to obtain an increase in yields and/or a reduc- tion in resource consumption, thus amortizing the impact per kg of consumed feed. This mitigation strategy is limited by the fact that the farmer cannot intervene directly in this field, since the analysed farm depends entirely on the external purchase of feed. What would therefore be needed is a joint action between stakeholders of

the supply chain, which should be undertaken at a collective level from farmers since the scarce availability of land for self-production of feed is a common feature to most of the poultry holdings in Italy (Eurostat, 2020). The same should be done for the mitigation of the impact due to pullets rearing.

Concerning the impact categories strongly affected by manure- related emissions, namely PM, TA and TE, a considerable mitigation could be obtained by deploying abatement technologies in farm facilities, such as ventilation systems that allow rapid drying of the excreta after deposition or air scrubbers, which have been proven to be a good solution in particular for ammonia (Van der Heyden et al., 2015). However, this latter implementation is unlikely to be applied in organic farms' barns since they use natural ventilation systems. A careful fulfilment of protein requirements has also been identified as a potential mitigation strategy (Vesela et al., 2016), so that it can simultaneously avoid protein overdoses in the ration, reducing in- puts of protein ingredients, and limiting the nitrogen excretion of animals. A weakness of organic farming in this sense is that re- strictions exist on the use of some ingredients, such as synthetic amino acids, making it more difficult to accurately meet nutritional requirements of the animals.

Another mitigation opportunity could be the partial replace- ment of traditional protein sources with alternative ones (insects and worms). Recently, many studies have explored this topic in the poultry sector, highlighting promising results both in production and environmental sustainability terms (Khan, 2018). Further research efforts are needed to better investigate the impacts that alternative protein meals can have if implemented regularly and in large quantities in laying hens' diets.

Finally, even if manure is collected only once at the end of the cycle, its energetic valorisation through anaerobic digestion and biogas production would be a viable option for further mitigation. By providing renewable energy, biogas production from manure could contribute to the "eco-functional intensification" of organic farming (Siegmer et al., 2015) and also foster the circular economy concept in agriculture (Toop et al., 2017). Several LCA studies (Uusitalo et al., 2014; Bacenetti and Fiala, 2015; Ingrao et al., 2019) have shown how a biogas plant fed with animal manure can reach a negative value (i.e., environmental benefits) for the Climate Change impact category when are considered the credits for the avoided traditional storage of manure. Anaerobic digestion of poultry manure would also be positive from an agronomic point of view since the anaerobic digestion increases the proportion of ammo- niacal nitrogen on total nitrogen in digestate (Roos et al., 2018) and, consequently, its effectiveness in the substitution of mineral fer- tilizers. Moreover, poultry manure, if digested together with other biomasses (to avoid/reduce the risk of high ammonia concentration into the digesters) has an excellent energy potential both because it normally has a higher volatile solids content than other livestock effluents and it presents also a higher CH4 yield per kg of volatile solids (Garcia et al., 2019).

Due to the lack of information, this study did not include the effect of the system on land use, water use, biodiversity and carbon sequestration, therefore the discussion may present restrictions. Future analyses should deepen the relationships between organic laying hens farming and these topics in a wider environmental perspective. Also, a challenge for future studies is to encompass the environmental dimension of sustainability, creating a connection with animal welfare aspects: several studies have investigated and compared the influence that different rearing systems, feeding management and housing conditions can have on animal welfare (EFSA, 2005; Nicol et al., 2017). Nevertheless, these aspects are not taken into consideration when carrying out an LCA analysis. Recently, a preliminary approach to include animal welfare in a social-LCA model for chicken production was proposed (Tallentire
et al., 2019). Next step could be to combine this approach together with the LCA in a broader life cycle framework.

Also, pullets production and emissions related to manure contributed significantly. As concerns the substances contribution analysis of CC, the results obtained in this study are in accordance with Dekker et al. (2011) and Wiedemann and McGahan (2010), while other authors (e.g. Abín et al., 2018) identified N20 instead of CO2 as the main greenhouse gas emitted in terms of CO2 eq from the egg production system.

With the awareness that some of the variability observed between different LCA studies is due to methodological choices, the result still shows the analysed farm to have a good environmental performance in terms of carbon foot- print. The farm's productivity and the avoidance of some inputs that normally weigh heavily from an environmental point of view in conventional systems, such as mineral fertilizers and imported feed ingredients, have contributed to achieving this result. The goal must be to optimize this consumption without sacrificing productivity. However, the environmental mitigation on feed production must also take place beyond the farm boundaries: it is essential that sustainable production starts from crops, adopting strategies to obtain an increase in yields and/or a reduc- tion in resource consumption, thus amortizing the impact per kg of consumed feed. This mitigation strategy is limited by the fact that the farmer cannot intervene directly in this field, since the analysed farm depends entirely on the external purchase of feed. What would therefore be needed is a joint action between stakeholders of

the supply chain, which should be undertaken at a collective level from farmers since the scarce availability of land for self-production of feed is a common feature to most of the poultry holdings in Italy (Eurostat, 2020). The same should be done for the mitigation of the impact due to pullets rearing.

Concerning the impact categories strongly affected by manure- related emissions, namely PM, TA and TE, a considerable mitigation could be obtained by deploying abatement technologies in farm facilities, such as ventilation systems that allow rapid drying of the excreta after deposition or air scrubbers, which have been proven to be a good solution in particular for ammonia (Van der Heyden et al., 2015). However, this latter implementation is unlikely to be applied in organic farms' barns since they use natural ventilation systems. A careful fulfilment of protein requirements has also been identified as a potential mitigation strategy (Vesela et al., 2016), so that it can simultaneously avoid protein overdoses in the ration, reducing in- puts of protein ingredients, and limiting the nitrogen excretion of animals. A weakness of organic farming in this sense is that re- strictions exist on the use of some ingredients, such as synthetic amino acids, making it more difficult to accurately meet nutritional requirements of the animals.

Another mitigation opportunity could be the partial replace- ment of traditional protein sources with alternative ones (insects and worms). Recently, many studies have explored this topic in the poultry sector, highlighting promising results both in production and environmental sustainability terms (Khan, 2018). Further research efforts are needed to better investigate the impacts that alternative protein meals can have if implemented regularly and in large quantities in laying hens' diets.

Moreover, poultry manure, if digested together with other biomasses (to avoid/reduce the risk of high ammonia concentration into the digesters) has an excellent energy potential both because it normally has a higher volatile solids content than other livestock effluents and it presents also a higher CH4 yield per kg of volatile solids (Garcia et al., 2019).

et al., 2019). Next step could be to combine this approach together with the LCA in a broader life cycle framework.

15: The environmental impact of organic egg production was eval- uated by the LCA approach. The results show that feed production is the major impact contributor for all the evaluated categories. Consequently, a greater effort in reducing the environmental burden of egg production should be made first of all in the opti- mization of feed consumption on farm and in the crop production phase.

The outcomes of the sensitivity analysis point out the impor- tance of allocation choice in defining the environmental load of eggs when considering co-products of the system, as it happens for other products of animal origin, and also highlight that the pro- duction efficiency of the rearing system plays an important role in determining positive environmental performance.

Each rearing system (conventional or organic) has peculiar positive and negative characteristics from an environmental point of view, and some variability can be observed across and within the different systems. Despite some criticisms that organic farming receives in literature, the farm analysed in this study has a good productivity, which was decisive for determining the environ- mental impact. The impact largely depends on farmers' manage- ment choices, rather than on the adopted rearing system and, a priori, there is not a best farming system for all circumstances. In any case, opportunities for further mitigation exist at the farm level in terms of feeding strategies, manure management and measures related to the housing system. Efforts at the supply chain level are instead needed to mitigate the impact of feed production and supply.

As this represents, to the authors' knowledge, the first LCA analysis on eggs in Italy, a starting point for future studies is to expand the sample to other farms and explore with comparative LCA different rearing systems, in order to directly compare the performance of organic and conventional production in the same context. Even some alternative scenarios with the mitigation so- lutions proposed in this study could be explored, evaluating their feasibility and social (animal welfare in particular), environmental and economic performance.
